title: How Two Lines Solve XOR
concept: A visual walkthrough of the XOR fix: two hidden perceptrons each draw their own line across the plane, and an output perceptron combines the two answers to carve out exactly the XOR pattern.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the XOR-solving network, what does the hidden layer do before the output perceptron sees anything?

options.text: Draws its own line(s), splitting the four points into groups
Deletes the false answers from the truth table
Randomly guesses without looking at the input

exercises.explain: Each hidden perceptron draws its own straight-line split first; the output perceptron combines those results.

cards.title: Grab two rulers
cards.body: Picture the four XOR points plotted on a square: (0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1). One ruler (one perceptron) can only draw one line through that square. What can two rulers do?

cards.title: Each hidden perceptron draws one line
cards.body: In the XOR-solving network, each of the two hidden perceptrons draws its own straight line across the same square of points — splitting the four corners into "which side of my line" groups.

cards.title: Two nets catching different fish
cards.body: Imagine two fishing nets cast across the same pond at different angles. Neither net alone catches only the fish you want. But look at where both nets overlap, and suddenly you've isolated exactly the fish you were after.

cards.title: Walking the XOR grid
cards.body: Line A (from an OR-style perceptron) cuts off only the (0,0) corner as "different." Line B (from a NAND-style perceptron) cuts off only the (1,1) corner as "different." The output perceptron then asks: "Are you on the shared, agreed-upon side of BOTH lines?" Only (0,1) and (1,0) — the true XOR points — pass that test.

cards.title: You don't need endless lines
cards.body: It's tempting to think a tricky pattern needs a huge tangle of lines. XOR — one of the simplest "impossible" patterns for a single perceptron — needs just two. More complicated patterns need more, but two is the minimum here.

left.text:
Splits the square in half
Always leaves XOR mixed together

right.text:
Each line trims one corner
Overlap isolates the true points

exercises.prompt: In the XOR-solving network, what does each hidden perceptron do before the output perceptron combines anything?

options.text: Draws its own straight line, splitting the four points into two groups
Directly outputs the final XOR answer
Waits for the output perceptron to draw its line first

exercises.explain: Both hidden perceptrons independently draw their own line across the same points.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Solving XOR with two lines means each individual line, by itself, correctly separates true from false.
exercises.explain: False — neither line alone separates XOR correctly. It's the combination, the overlap of both lines, that isolates the true points.

exercises.sentence: Two hidden perceptrons each cut off one "different" corner, and the output perceptron looks for where both cuts ___ to find the true XOR points.
exercises.explain: The true XOR points are exactly where the two lines' "agreed" regions overlap.

exercises.prompt: Which best describes why two lines succeed where one line fails on XOR?

options.text: One line can only ever create two regions total; two lines combined can carve out a more specific third shape
Two lines are simply drawn thicker than one line
Two lines work because they use bigger numbers

exercises.explain: A single line always leaves the plane split into just two regions. Combining two lines lets you carve out a more specific shape — exactly what XOR needs.

exercises.prompt: You're shown a new made-up pattern where the true points form a small diamond shape in the middle of a square, surrounded by false points. Based on what you learned about XOR, what's the most reasonable guess about solving it?

options.text: It probably needs more than one line too — likely several hidden perceptrons whose lines combine to trim the shape down to that diamond
It's impossible for any number of perceptrons, since only XOR can ever be solved this way
A single perceptron can do it, since a diamond is a simple shape
You'd have to redefine what "true" and "false" mean for the pattern to make sense

exercises.explain: Exactly the right instinct! Any shape that a single straight line can't isolate is a job for combining multiple perceptrons' lines — XOR was just the simplest example of that idea.
